content.text: Now I'll write the HTML content to the blackboard file.

input.content:
CBT Submission Package

Rossland Works

A coworking space for Rossland's remote workers, entrepreneurs, and small businesses — developed in partnership with Columbia Basin Trust.

Location
Rossland, BC
Space
1,800 sq ft
Capital Required
$61,500 – $117,000
CBT Funding Sought
$50,000 – $115,000
Prepared
March 2026

This document is the submission guide for the Rossland Works CBT funding application. It maps the supporting documents, summarizes the financial case, and gives the founder a clear sequence of steps before the formal application is filed.

Submission Package — Three Annexes

Submit all three documents together to CBT's Impact Investment Fund. Each stands alone but is designed to be read as a set.

A
Business Plan

Market analysis, community need, membership structure, operations model, and CBT partnership rationale. Covers Rossland and Trail context, target member profiles, and C3 governance option.

business-plan.html
B
Financial Model

Three-year projections across three scenarios. Startup capital and funding source tables. All KPI figures verified against detailed tables. Revenue stated net of GST. Add-ons revenue itemized by component.

financial-model.html
C
Launch Roadmap

Four-phase execution plan with go/no-go gates at Month 3 and Month 6. CBT milestone reporting schedule, risk register with mitigations, 14-item pre-opening checklist.

launch-roadmap.html
Financial Scenarios at a Glance

All scenarios assume 1,800 sq ft at $1,800/mo, a part-time community manager starting at 80 hrs/mo, and a 7-month pre-opening period. Year 1 figures cover the first full operating year. All revenue is net of GST.

Conservative
$46,412
Year 1 Revenue
Y1 Net($12,508)
Y2 Net+$5,960
Y3 Net+$22,864
Avg Members Y115
Break-EvenMonth 22
Realistic — Base Case
$87,632
Year 1 Revenue
Y1 Net+$28,712
Y2 Net+$63,228
Y3 Net+$86,336
Avg Members Y125
Break-EvenMonth 6-8*
Optimistic
$137,056
Year 1 Revenue
Y1 Net+$74,836
Y2 Net+$114,232
Y3 Net+$144,980
Avg Members Y140
Break-EvenMonth 3-4

*Realistic break-even conditional on 15 founding members signed before opening. CBT loan repayment not included in net figures — see Annex B for detail.

Funding Strategy
SourceTypeTarget
CBT Impact Investment Fund — primary application; low/no interest; community benefit criteria met by economic development and remote work retention rationaleRepayable loan$50,000–$75,000
CBT Working Capital Loan — supplements Impact Investment Fund or serves as bridge; faster approval cycleRepayable loanUp to $40,000
Founding Member Pre-Sales — 15 charter members at $75/mo x 12 months; non-dilutive; also validates demand for CBT applicationRevenue$13,500
REDI Grant (RDKB) — Regional Economic Development Initiative; investigate eligibility with RDKB Economic DevelopmentGrant (if eligible)$5,000–$20,000
Founder Investment — demonstrates commitment; reduces loan-to-equity ratioEquity$10,000–$20,000
Columbia Basin Credit Union — parallel path if CBT is delayed; open relationship before you need a decisionConventional loanSupplement as needed
Combined funding capacity$78,500–$168,500
Pre-Submission Checklist

Items marked Done are complete in the prepared package. The rest require action by the founder before submitting.

  • Business plan written (Annex A)Market analysis, membership structure, operations, CBT rationale, and risk register complete.
    Done
  • Financial model prepared and verified (Annex B)Three scenarios with verified KPIs, startup capital table, funding sources, GST treatment noted, add-ons revenue itemized by component.
    Done
  • Launch roadmap prepared (Annex C)Four-phase plan with milestone gates, CBT reporting schedule, risk register, and pre-opening checklist complete.
    Done
  • Register the legal entityIncorporate a BC corporation or Community Contribution Company (C3) and obtain a CRA business number. Budget $1,500-3,000. CBT cannot process a loan application without a registered entity.
    Do First
  • Book introductory meeting with CBT Basin AdvisorPresent Annexes A and B. Get informal feedback before the formal application enters the queue. A letter of interest from CBT accelerates review significantly. cbt.org/businesses
    Do First
  • Run community demand survey2 weeks, targeting Rossland and Trail remote workers. Target 50+ responses and 15+ soft commitments. Attach results as an appendix to the CBT application — they are evidence of community support.
    Do First
  • Collect letters of supportCity of Rossland Economic Development, Rossland Chamber, RDKB. Even brief acknowledgement letters carry weight in CBT's community benefit evaluation criteria.
    Do First
  • Shortlist 3 candidate spacesDocument floor plans, lease rates, and landlord flexibility. Confirm layouts accommodate 12+ open desks, meeting room, and 1-2 private offices. Attach shortlist to the application.
    Do First
  • Submit CBT applicationFull package: Annexes A, B, C plus survey results, support letters, space shortlist, and entity documents. Apply for Working Capital Loan simultaneously. Allow 60-90 days for review.
    After Prep
  • Inquire about REDI Grant and open Columbia Basin Credit Union relationshipNon-repayable REDI grant if eligible. CBCU as a parallel financing path. Open both relationships before you need a decision from either.
    Parallel Track
Top Risks for CBT Review
High
Small population — Rossland has ~4,100 residentsAddressed in business plan: meeting room and event revenue draws from Trail (~8,000 residents, 15 min away) and Warfield. Non-member revenue is modelled conservatively. The realistic scenario is defensible with Trail market access.
High
Demand risk — unproven coworking market in RosslandMitigated by demand survey and founding member pre-sales (15 sign-ups before opening). Present survey results and pre-sales data with the application. Pre-sales provide $13,500 in pre-revenue and are direct proof of demand.
Medium
Seasonality — some Rossland activity is ski-dependentMember base targets year-round remote workers, not seasonal ski workers. Trail corporate accounts, virtual memberships, and summer programming provide off-season baseline. Conservative scenario accounts for seasonal softness.
Low
Competition — no coworking space currently exists in RosslandNo direct competitor as of March 2026. Nearest comparable is in Castlegar. First-mover position with Red Mountain Resort proximity is a durable local advantage.
Recommended Sequence
1
Week 1-2
Launch demand survey and register legal entity
Start the survey immediately — 2 weeks of data. Incorporate the entity in parallel. C3 structure may strengthen CBT application. Budget $1,500-3,000 for BC counsel familiar with C3 structures.
2
Week 3-4
CBT Basin Advisor meeting — present Annexes A and B
Bring preliminary survey results. Ask for informal feedback before formal submission. Advisors know what CBT reviewers prioritize — use this meeting to learn that before filing.
3
Week 4-6
Collect support letters and shortlist candidate spaces
Contact City of Rossland, Rossland Chamber, and RDKB. Visit properties and document lease rates. Both inputs are standard CBT requirements and take time to gather.
4
Week 6-8
Submit CBT Impact Investment Fund application
Attach all three annexes, survey results, support letters, space shortlist, and entity documents. File Working Capital Loan application simultaneously. CBT review: 60-90 days.
5
Month 3 — CBT Decision
Go/No-Go: sign lease conditional on funding confirmation
If approved: sign lease and begin fit-out per Annex C. If delayed: activate Columbia Basin Credit Union bridge loan. The launch roadmap takes over from this point with a week-by-week pre-launch plan.
On the CBT Community Benefit Test

CBT's Impact Investment Fund is a repayable loan — it requires demonstrable community or economic benefit to Basin residents. The strongest angle for Rossland Works: enabling remote workers to stay in or relocate to Rossland rather than commuting to Trail or leaving the region for larger cities. Frame every CBT conversation around that story. A coworking space that keeps five professional families in Rossland is a compelling Basin-benefit case. Pair this with the demand survey data and founding member pre-sales, and the application has a strong factual foundation.

Rossland Works
CBT Submission Package — March 2026 | Annexes A, B, C included
All revenue figures net of GST.
CBT loan repayment not modelled in projections.

content.text: The write operation returned a "Stream closed" error. Let me verify whether the file was actually written successfully by checking what's on the blackboard.

The MCP blackboard tools are returning "Stream closed" errors, which indicates a connectivity issue with the blackboard MCP server. The write operation could not be completed successfully. The blackboard service appears to be unavailable or disconnected at this time.

To resolve this, the blackboard MCP server connection would need to be restarted or re-established before the file can be written. The HTML content itself is ready and correct — the issue is purely with the transport layer between the agent and the blackboard service.
